reason: Automation as an alternative to higher wages contradicts the claim that higher wages will simply reduce teen hours while preserving their jobs.
Con14 claims companies may outsource jobs, threatening teen employment and thus undermining the premise that teens will still have jobs to work fewer hours.
Con14b argues outsourcing reduces American jobs, challenging the premise that teens will retain employment to benefit from higher wages.
Con70 suggests price pass‑through could reduce demand and cut teen jobs, undermining the premise that teens will stay employed to work fewer hours.
Con70b similarly predicts widespread cost‑pass‑through, threatening teen employment and thus the premise of the pro claim.
Con12 asserts higher labor costs force firms to hire fewer workers, directly challenging the premise that teens can keep jobs and reduce hours.
Con76 cites empirical drops in teenage employment after a wage hike, undermining the premise that teens will retain jobs to benefit from higher pay.
Con76b reports that the 2009 wage increase cut teenage employment, attacking the premise of continued teen employment.
B suggests higher wages may lead to automation, undermining A's premise that increased wages keep teens in school by providing jobs.
Con argues outsourcing reduces domestic jobs, challenging the premise that higher wages will keep teens employed and in school.
Con claims outsourcing cuts American jobs, undermining the premise that a wage rise will provide teens with jobs to stay in school.
Con says a wage hike is merely a bandage, arguing the conclusion that it reduces dropout does not follow even if wages rise.
Con asserts firms will hire fewer workers if wages rise, undermining the premise that teens will retain jobs to stay in school.
Con provides evidence of a sharp drop in teenage employment after a wage increase, challenging the premise that higher wages keep teens in school.
Con states the 2009 wage hike significantly reduced teenage employment, directly undermining the pro argument's premise.
B argues that higher wages will lead employers to replace clerks with kiosks, so even if wages rise, workers may not benefit.
B challenges the premise that raising the federal minimum wage will benefit workers by suggesting it could cause outsourcing, reducing those benefits
A’s premise that productivity exceeds wage growth challenges con14’s premise that firms will outsource because wages are too high.
A’s productivity claim undermines the premise that outsourcing to cheaper labor is necessary, thus weakening con14b’s argument.
B shows that even if wages rise (A's premise), price pass‑through could erase workers' net benefit, so A's conclusion doesn't follow
B shows that higher wages may reduce hiring, so workers may not benefit despite wage increase
B shows that a minimum‑wage rise can cause job loss, so even if wages rise (A's premise), the claimed overall benefit to workers does not necessarily follow.
Con70 challenges the premise that productivity gains allow firms to absorb higher wages without passing costs to consumers
Con70b similarly attacks the premise that firms can absorb wage hikes, suggesting they will pass costs to consumers
Con12 disputes the premise that higher wages can be afforded without reducing employment
Con76 provides empirical evidence that a wage increase coincided with a sharp drop in teenage employment, undermining the productivity premise
Con76b asserts that the 2009 wage hike reduced teenage employment, challenging the premise of affordable wage growth
con12 claims higher wages cut hiring, challenging the premise that growth outpacing wage hikes means no employment harm
con76 shows teenage employment fell after the wage rise, demonstrating that the conclusion of pro4b does not follow even if growth outpaced wages
con76b asserts the 2009 increase reduced teen jobs, directly refuting the implication that outpacing growth prevents employment loss
con18 questions the need to pay higher wages by suggesting automation, challenging the premise that firms must pay low wages to staff clerks
Pro's claim of no price increase directly contradicts con's claim that fast‑food restaurants would pass on almost 100% of wage costs to consumers.
Pro's finding of little or no price rise opposes con's assertion that other firms would fully pass on higher labor costs to consumers.
Con argues that outsourcing would occur, showing the pro’s claim that minimum‑wage hikes are harmless does not follow even if GDP grew.
Con claims outsourcing reduces American jobs, challenging the inference that minimum‑wage raises are economically benign.
Con attacks the premise that a wage hike solves problems, labeling it merely a “bandage,” thereby undermining the pro’s implicit claim of effectiveness.
Con shows that price pass‑through could occur, indicating the pro’s conclusion that minimum‑wage hikes are harmless does not necessarily follow.
Similar to con70, it argues cost pass‑through by other firms, undercutting the pro’s inference of no negative impact.
Con asserts higher labor costs reduce hiring, challenging the pro’s implication that minimum‑wage increases are without adverse effects.
Con provides evidence of a sharp teen‑employment drop after a wage hike, showing the pro’s claim does not guarantee employment stability.
Con’s statement that the 2009 increase cut teenage employment directly undercuts the pro’s inference of no harmful consequences.
The con argument questions the relevance of higher wages by suggesting automation can replace clerks, attacking the premise that workers need higher pay.
It claims companies will outsource to cheaper labor, challenging the premise that raising the minimum wage won’t cause job loss.
It argues outsourcing reduces American jobs, undermining the premise that a higher minimum wage benefits domestic employment.
It asserts a wage hike is merely a superficial fix, challenging the premise that raising the minimum wage meaningfully addresses economic problems.
It predicts full cost pass‑through to consumers, undermining the premise that a higher minimum wage benefits workers without adverse price effects.
It extends the cost‑pass‑through argument to all firms, similarly undermining the premise of net benefit from a wage increase.
It claims businesses cannot afford to hire as many employees if wages rise, attacking the feasibility premise of the pro argument.
It provides empirical evidence that a past minimum‑wage hike reduced teenage employment, showing the pro conclusion doesn’t follow even if its premise were true.
The automation argument attacks the premise that a higher minimum wage is needed by showing that technology can replace low‑skill clerks regardless of wage level.
The outsourcing claim undermines the premise that inflation‑adjusted wages alone determine appropriate pay, suggesting firms will move jobs abroad if wages rise.
The argument that outsourcing reduces American jobs attacks the same premise that wage‑inflation alignment is sufficient, highlighting a potential adverse effect.
The claim that fast‑food restaurants will pass 100% of higher labor costs to consumers attacks the premise that a higher wage is without broader economic harm.
The broader claim that many firms will fully shift higher wages onto prices challenges the premise that inflation‑adjusted wage levels are a benign policy.
The argument that businesses cannot afford to hire as many employees attacks the premise that raising the minimum wage is feasible based solely on inflation adjustments.
[symmetric rebuttal] Automation as an alternative to higher wages contradicts the claim that higher wages will simply reduce teen hours while preserving their jobs.
[symmetric rebuttal] Pro's claim of no price increase directly contradicts con's claim that fast‑food restaurants would pass on almost 100% of wage costs to consumers.
[symmetric rebuttal] Pro's finding of little or no price rise opposes con's assertion that other firms would fully pass on higher labor costs to consumers.
